text: We will start with a VATS left upper lobectomy, making the standard incisions with my approach.
We first make a single camera incision in about the eighth intercostal space in the posterior axillary line for the camera itself.
The camera is a 30-degree, 10-millimeter scope, although alternatively a 5-millimeter scope may be used.
After making the first camera incision, I typically use a posterior small incision for assistance and for placement of the staplers.
This is in the 8th or 9th intercostal space, just below the scapular tip.
Finally, the main working incision, which is about 3 to 3.5 centimeters in length, is in about the mid-axillary line,
overlying the fourth intercostal space which should bring you down on the superior pulmonary vein.
This inset demonstrates the typical visualization of the anterior portion of the hilum with the superior pulmonary vein.
Oftentimes during the operation, both in the front of the hilum and the back of the hilum, careful dissection of the hilar lymph nodes provides adequate nodal staging but also provides a critical
visualization of the surrounding anatomic structures.
This is a hilar node being carefully dissected with scissors in the simulation model.
Alternatively, blunt dissection or energy may be used in a live model.
We are attempting to expose the superior and inferior edge of the superior pulmonary vein.
I'm often careful to also identify the inferior pulmonary vein
to avoid inadvertent injury to that.
This inset demonstrates an in vivo model of the superior pulmonary vein with the origin of the left lower lobe vein in the bottom right corner.
We continue the dissection of the left hilar lymph nodes that are allowing us to demonstrate some of the surrounding structures including the bronchus and the vein period.
The inset below is an in vivo model of a dissection coming around the superior pulmonary vein from below.
This can be done with a right angle through the anterior working incision.
Alternatively, as demonstrated in the in vivo model, a straight clamp can be placed through the most posterior incision to allow for circumferential mobilization of the superior pulmonary vein.
The stapler is then generally delivered through the most posterior incision, as demonstrated in both the in vivo model as well as the simulation model.
Great care has to be taken to avoid the tip engaging the pulmonary artery on the superior side of the pulmonary vein to avoid injury to that structure.
Once the vein is mobilized, it is divided, typically with the vascular load of the stapler.
This then should allow exposure of the bronchus and pulmonary artery in behind the vein itself.
As stated, the stapling location for the superior pulmonary vein is often through the most posterior incision, which gives generally a good angle.
Other surgeons may prefer alternative sites for placement of the stapler, but it is generally not through the working port.
because that angle does not provide the right angle for division of the vessel.
Now with retraction through the posterior incision, the continued dissection is performed through the anterior incision.
Here we are continuing with the nodal dissection, removing more peripheral hilar nodes off of the pulmonary artery.
exposing again the bronchus, including the left upper lobe bronchus and the left lower lobe bronchus, as depicted here in this picture.
Oftentimes from the anterior side you cannot see the left main stem bronchus, but you can often demonstrate the lower lobe and upper lobe takeoff as it comes underneath the pulmonary artery.
Invariably, there is a left hilar station 11 lymph node that overlies the junction between the left upper lobe bronchus and left lower lobe bronchus.
And directly behind this lymph node is the pulmonary artery as it continues through the fissure.
Careful dissection of this lymph node.
provides much better visualization of these anatomic structures as the bronchus is mobilized near the pulmonary artery.

It is possible to again dissect with a right angle retractor from the anterior ports or to utilize a straight instrument to dissect around the upper lobe bronchus to mobilize the entire upper lobe bronchus.
With the simulation model, we are using a right-angled instrument.
As you can see in the inset of the in vivo model, I am using a straight instrument.
Again, the stapler passes through the most posterior incision to allow for a straight angle around the left upper lobe bronchus.
This is often one of the most precarious maneuvers in a left upper lobectomy.
And as you can see by the in vivo model, we have used a red rubber catheter as a guide to be able to advance the stapler.
Once the red rubber is removed, we then divide the bronchus after confirming adequate ventilation of the lower lobe.

With division of the bronchus, we should now have better visualization of the entire pulmonary artery as it continues along with the upper lobe branches as well as with the continuation of the pulmonary artery to the lower lobe.
Care has to be taken when retracting the lung upward to avoid torsion
on the vessels themselves.
As is typical with the left upper lobe, there is a variable number of pulmonary artery branches.
There is invariably a large truncal branch at the beginning which may even be visualized prior to division of the bronchus.
It is not absolutely necessary to divide the bronchus before dividing the truncal vessels.
It just depends on its location.
This inset demonstrates the pulmonary artery branches once the bronchus has been divided and correlates it with the simulation model similarly with the exposed pulmonary artery branches.
It is imperative to identify the location of the pulmonary artery when one is dividing the fissure to avoid inadvertent injury to the pulmonary artery within the fissure itself.
The steps of the division of the fissure can be performed at any time during the operation
as a matter of convenience and for the adequacy of exposure of the other vessels.
Once the medial portion of the fissure is divided, it allows you to mobilize the lung posteriorly to expose multiple branches that can often be present in a left upper lobe.
Here in the simulation model, we have completed the majority of the medial portion of the fissure, exposing two additional lingular artery branches to the upper lobe.
These can be carefully mobilized with blunt dissection and sharp dissection.
The deployment site of this stapler is variable given the angle needed for the lingular vessels.
At times this can be from the posterior approach, although alternatively it can also be through the anterior incision.
There are still additional posterior branches of the pulmonary artery to the upper lobe.
that are individually mobilized and typically divided with the vascular load of the stapler.
There may be additional fissure tissue posteriorly which can be divided with a thicker load of the stapler once the vasculature has been completely divided.
As you can see on the right of the screen, this is the pulmonary artery as it continues on to the lower lobe.
with multiple pulmonary artery branches having been divided.
Here we demonstrate a total of four branches with some of these individual branches being bifurcated at their site.
I'd like to thank you for the opportunity to help educate you on a VATS lobectomy.
I wish you the best of luck in your surgical training.
